children.name: Meles
children.description: The true badgers of the Palearctic — powerfully built, short-legged diggers with the iconic black-and-white striped face. Eurasian Badgers are the largest land predators native to Britain and among the most social of all mustelids, living in multi-family clans in elaborate underground tunnel systems called setts that may be used continuously for centuries. Their foraging leaves characteristic conical snout-holes across pasture, and an earthy, musky sett on a woodland edge is one of the most evocative smells of the European night.

children.name: Meles meles
children.description: Meles meles (Eurasian Badger) is a badgers species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Meles leucurus
children.description: Meles leucurus (Asian Badger) is a badgers species found in Asia region.

children.name: Meles anakuma
children.description: Meles anakuma (Japanese Badger) is a badgers species found in Asia region.

children.name: Meles canescens
children.description: A medium-sized badger of the Caucasus, Anatolia, the Levant, and Central Asia, formerly considered a subspecies of the Eurasian Badger. It has paler facial stripes and a greyer body than M. meles, and occupies diverse habitats from Mediterranean maquis to subalpine meadows.

children.name: Arctonyx
children.description: Hog badgers — large, pig-snouted badgers of South and East Asia with a distinctive white throat and elongated, flexible snout used to root through soil and leaf litter for invertebrates, tubers, and small vertebrates. They are the largest members of the badger lineage and are largely solitary and nocturnal, inhabiting forests and grasslands from northeast India to China.

children.name: Arctonyx collaris
children.description: Arctonyx collaris (Greater Hog Badger) is a badgers species found in Asia region.

children.name: Arctonyx albogularis
children.description: Arctonyx albogularis (Northern Hog Badger) is a badgers species found in Asia region.

children.name: Arctonyx hoevenii
children.description: A hog badger restricted to the mountains of Sumatra, distinguished by its smaller size and proportionally longer tail than its mainland relatives. It is poorly known and rarely encountered.

children.name: Mellivora
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Honey Badger — pound for pound one of the most ferocious animals on Earth. The Honey Badger has loose, thick skin that allows it to twist around and bite an attacker holding it from behind, and it is resistant to bee stings, porcupine quills, and many snake venoms. It has been reliably observed raiding beehives, eating cobras, and tearing open concrete enclosures in zoos. Its fearlessness is not bravado; it has almost no natural predators.

children.name: Mellivora capensis
children.description: Mellivora capensis (Honey Badger) is a honey badger species found in Africa and Asia regions.

children.name: Gulo
children.description: The wolverine — the largest terrestrial mustelid, built like a small bear with the temperament of something considerably larger. Wolverines range enormous territories across the boreal forests and Arctic tundra of Scandinavia, Russia, and North America, scavenging frozen carcasses with jaws strong enough to crush bone and even frozen meat. In Scandinavia they are a totemic animal of the wilderness, feared and respected by reindeer herders whose semi-domestic herds they occasionally prey upon.

children.name: Gulo gulo
children.description: Gulo gulo (Wolverine) is a wolverine species found in Europe and Asia and North America regions.

children.name: Martes
children.description: The martens — slender, tree-climbing mustelids with semi-retractable claws and a bounding gait through forest canopy. The European Pine Marten is a forest specialist that has recovered strongly in parts of Scandinavia and Britain, partly because it outcompetes the introduced Grey Squirrel. The Sable was so relentlessly hunted for its extraordinarily soft, dark fur that it drove Russian eastward expansion across Siberia; Cossack expeditions were essentially sable-hunting enterprises.

children.name: Martes martes
children.description: Martes martes (European Pine Marten) is a martens species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Martes foina
children.description: Martes foina (Beech Marten) is a martens species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Martes zibellina
children.description: Martes zibellina (Sable) is a martens species found in Asia and Europe regions.

children.name: Martes melampus
children.description: Martes melampus (Japanese Marten) is a martens species found in Asia region.

children.name: Martes flavigula
children.description: Martes flavigula (Yellow-throated Marten) is a martens species found in Asia region.

children.name: Martes americana
children.description: A lithe, agile tree-climber of North American boreal forests, closely related to the European Pine Marten and occupying a similar ecological niche. It is an important furbearer whose populations declined sharply with trapping and old-growth logging but have partially recovered with forest succession; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Martes caurina
children.description: Recently split from the American Marten, this species occupies coastal forests from Alaska to California. It is genetically distinct and faces threats from habitat fragmentation and climate change in its restricted Pacific range.

children.name: Martes gwatkinsii
children.description: A rare, dark marten of the Western Ghats of southern India, the only marten species found south of the Himalayas. It inhabits montane evergreen and shola forests above 1000 m and is threatened by habitat loss. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Neogale
children.description: New World weasels, recently separated from Mustela based on molecular evidence. The American Mink and the Neotropical weasels form a distinct clade that split from Eurasian weasels millions of years ago. The Sea Mink of the Atlantic coast was hunted to extinction in the 19th century for its fur.

children.name: Neogale frenata
children.description: The most widely distributed weasel in the Americas, ranging from southern Canada to Bolivia. It is a highly adaptable predator of small mammals and birds, and like the stoat it turns white in winter in northern populations; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Neogale africana
children.description: A little-known weasel of the Amazon Basin, found in lowland tropical forests from Brazil to Ecuador. It is small and slender with reddish-brown fur and a long tail. Very little is known of its ecology.

children.name: Neogale felipei
children.description: One of the rarest carnivores in the Neotropics, known from only a handful of specimens from Colombia and Ecuador. It is small with dark brown fur and a short tail, and occurs in premontane forests. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Neogale vison
children.description: A semiaquatic mustelid of North American waterways, widely introduced to Europe and South America where it has become highly invasive, causing declines in native water voles and seabirds through predation.

children.name: Neogale macrodon
children.description: A large mink of the Atlantic coast of New England and the Maritime Provinces, significantly larger than the American Mink. It was hunted to extinction for its exceptionally thick, luxurious fur by the late 19th century, with the last confirmed record from 1894 off the coast of Maine. Its extinction predates formal conservation legislation.

children.name: Mustela
children.description: Weasels, stoats, and polecats — the smallest mustelids and among the smallest carnivores. They are specialist hunters of small mammals, able to pursue mice and voles into their own burrows, and can take prey several times their own body weight. The stoat turns white in winter (becoming the 'ermine' of royal robes) — one of the few Palearctic mammals with a seasonal colour change — while the Least Weasel is the smallest carnivorous mammal on Earth.

children.name: Mustela nivalis
children.description: Mustela nivalis (Least Weasel) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe and Asia and North America and Africa regions.

children.name: Mustela erminea
children.description: Mustela erminea (Stoat) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe and Asia and North America regions.

children.name: Mustela putorius
children.description: Mustela putorius (European Polecat) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe region.

children.name: Mustela eversmannii
children.description: Mustela eversmannii (Steppe Polecat) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Mustela lutreola
children.description: Mustela lutreola (European Mink) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe region.

children.name: Mustela sibirica
children.description: Mustela sibirica (Siberian Weasel) is a weasels & polecats species found in Asia region.

children.name: Mustela itatsi
children.description: Mustela itatsi (Japanese Weasel) is a weasels & polecats species found in Asia region.

children.name: Mustela nigripes
children.description: North America's only native ferret, once considered extinct and now the subject of one of the most intensive recovery programmes on the continent, reintroduced to the Great Plains from a captive population of just 18 individuals. Virtually dependent on prairie dogs for food and burrows; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Mustela altaica
children.description: A high-altitude weasel of the steppes and rocky slopes from the Altai Mountains to the Himalayas and western China, preying chiefly on pikas, voles, and small birds. It is well adapted to cold, thin-air environments and is largely crepuscular; IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Mustela kathiah
children.description: A small Himalayan weasel with dark brown upperparts and a distinctive bright yellow belly, inhabiting montane forests from Nepal to central China. It is diurnal and terrestrial, feeding on rodents and insects.

children.name: Mustela lutreolina
children.description: A poorly known weasel restricted to the mountains of Java and Sumatra in Indonesia, where it inhabits montane forests above 1000 m. It has a dark brown body with pale yellow belly and a distinct white patch on the throat.

children.name: Mustela nudipes
children.description: A slender, reddish-brown weasel of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, with a striking white head and throat. It inhabits lowland rainforests and is largely crepuscular.

children.name: Mustela strigidorsa
children.description: A distinctive weasel of Southeast Asian forests with a bold silver-white stripe running from the crown to the base of the tail along the back. It is rare and poorly understood, known from scattered records across Nepal, northeast India, and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Mustela subpalmata
children.description: A small weasel endemic to the Nile Delta region of Egypt, where it is closely associated with human settlements and agricultural land. It was long considered a subspecies of the Least Weasel but is now recognised as a distinct species on genetic grounds.

children.name: Mustela haidarum
children.description: A recently described stoat-like weasel endemic to Haida Gwaii (the Queen Charlotte Islands) off British Columbia. It is larger than mainland stoats and has distinct genetic and skeletal features. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Mustela richardsonii
children.description: A North American ermine species split from the Holarctic stoat complex, found across much of Canada and the northern United States. It replaces Mustela erminea in the New World and has a distinct cranial morphology.

children.name: Vormela
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Marbled Polecat — one of the most strikingly patterned of all mustelids, with a mottled yellow-and-brown back and a black mask and belly. Found in arid grasslands and semi-deserts from southeastern Europe to China, it is a specialist predator of ground squirrels and voles. When threatened it arches its back, curls its tail, and displays the bold contrasting colours as a warning — possibly mimicking the warning display of skunks with which it shares common ancestry.

children.name: Vormela peregusna
children.description: Vormela peregusna (Marbled Polecat) is a weasels & polecats species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Lutra
children.description: The Eurasian Otter — a supremely adapted semi-aquatic hunter, with dense waterproof fur, webbed feet, closeable nostrils and ears, and eyes adapted to see underwater. Otters were almost extirpated from much of Europe by hunting and pesticide pollution in the 20th century but have made a spectacular comeback following the ban of organochlorine pesticides and legal protection. The sight of an otter sliding into a river at dusk is once again possible across Scandinavia and much of the British Isles.

children.name: Lutra lutra
children.description: Lutra lutra (Eurasian Otter) is a otters species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Lutra sumatrana
children.description: Lutra sumatrana (Hairy-nosed Otter) is a otters species found in Asia region.

children.name: Lutrogale
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Smooth-coated Otter, the largest of the Asian otters, distinguished by its sleek, shorter fur and distinctly flattened head. It is highly sociable, living and hunting cooperatively in family groups of up to a dozen individuals.

children.name: Lutrogale perspicillata
children.description: A large, sociable otter of South and Southeast Asian wetlands, with a sleek coat of short, velvety fur. It is the most social of all otters, hunting cooperatively in family groups and communicating with a complex repertoire of whistles and chirps. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Hydrictis
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Spotted-necked Otter, a medium-sized African otter distinguished by the mottled pattern of white and brown spots on its throat and chest. It is the most aquatic of African otters, with fully webbed feet and a lifestyle almost entirely confined to water.

children.name: Hydrictis maculicollis
children.description: A small, slender otter of sub-Saharan African lakes and rivers, easily recognised by the variable cream-and-brown spotting on its throat. It is fish-specialist with sensitive whiskers that detect water movements and is the most otter-like of all African otters in appearance and behaviour.

children.name: Aonyx
children.description: Clawless otters — Asian and African otters with reduced or absent claws and highly dexterous bare-skinned fingers, used to feel for crabs, molluscs, and frogs under rocks and in mud. The Asian Small-clawed Otter is the smallest otter species and one of the most social, living in extended family groups with complex vocal communication and cooperative foraging.

children.name: Aonyx cinerea
children.description: Aonyx cinerea (Asian Small-clawed Otter) is a otters species found in Asia region.

children.name: Aonyx capensis
children.description: Africa's largest otter, found across most of sub-Saharan Africa in rivers, lakes, and coastal waters. Its forepaws are almost hand-like, with naked palms and reduced nails, giving it extraordinary dexterity for catching crabs and other prey under rocks.

children.name: Aonyx congicus
children.description: A clawless otter of Central African rainforest rivers and swamps, ranging from Nigeria to the Congo Basin. It is similar to the African Clawless Otter but has a longer tail and lives in denser forests.

children.name: Lontra
children.description: New World river otters — four species distributed from Canada to Patagonia, filling the semi-aquatic freshwater predator niche vacated by Lutra in the Americas. They share the same streamlined body plan and dense waterproof fur as their Old World cousins but evolved independently in the Neotropics and along the Pacific coast.

children.name: Lontra canadensis
children.description: A highly mobile semi-aquatic predator of rivers, lakes, and coastal waters across North America, feeding on fish, crayfish, and amphibians. Extirpated from large parts of its range by trapping and pollution, it has been reintroduced successfully in many US states; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Lontra longicaudis
children.description: Widespread across freshwater habitats from Mexico to Uruguay, this otter is sensitive to water quality and human disturbance and has disappeared from many river systems due to hunting and habitat degradation. It is predominantly crepuscular and nocturnal; IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Lontra felina
children.description: The only exclusively marine otter of the Americas, found along the rocky coasts of Peru and Chile. It is a skilled diver that feeds on crustaceans, fish, and molluscs in the kelp forests of the cold Humboldt Current. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Lontra provocax
children.description: A rare otter of Chilean and Argentine Patagonian rivers and lakes, where it relies on mature riparian forests for denning. Its population has declined sharply due to habitat loss and historic hunting. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Pteronura
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Giant Otter — the world's longest mustelid, reaching up to 1.8 m from nose to tail-tip. Giant Otters are highly social, living in noisy, cooperative family groups of up to eight individuals that jointly defend territories along South American river systems. They were hunted almost to extinction for their plush, velvety pelts in the 20th century and remain among South America's most endangered large mammals.

children.name: Pteronura brasiliensis
children.description: The world's largest otter, inhabiting slow-moving rivers, lakes, and swamps of the Amazon and Orinoco basins. It is a diurnal, apex aquatic predator capable of catching caimans and anacondas. Populations were reduced by over 80% by the fur trade before protection; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Enhydra
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Sea Otter — the only fully marine mustelid and the heaviest member of the family. Sea Otters are renowned for using rocks as anvils to crack open shellfish, making them one of the few non-primate tool-using mammals. They spend almost their entire lives at sea, rarely coming ashore, and maintain body temperature without blubber by trapping air bubbles in the densest fur of any mammal — up to a million hairs per square centimetre.

children.name: Enhydra lutris
children.description: A keystone species of North Pacific kelp forest ecosystems, controlling sea urchin populations that would otherwise overgraze kelp. Hunted to near-extinction for its extraordinarily dense fur during the maritime fur trade; surviving populations in Alaska and California are recovering. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Taxidea
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the American Badger — the New World counterpart to the Eurasian Badger, though more closely related to martens than to Meles. It is a powerful, low-slung digger of open grasslands and shrublands, specialising in excavating ground squirrels, prairie dogs, and pocket gophers from their burrows with astonishing speed. Coyotes and American Badgers are well documented as cooperative hunters, one flushing prey above ground while the other waits at burrow entrances.

children.name: Taxidea taxus
children.description: A robust, flat-bodied burrowing predator of the Great Plains, Great Basin, and western grasslands of North America, with powerful forelimbs and long claws adapted for rapid digging. It is a solitary, largely nocturnal hunter of fossorial rodents and plays an important ecosystem role as a prolific excavator; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Pekania
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Fisher — a large, dark-furred marten of North American boreal and mixed forests. Despite its name the Fisher rarely eats fish; it is one of the few specialist predators of porcupines, attacking their unquilled face in a prolonged circling assault. Fishers were trapped to extinction across much of New England and the Midwest for their luxurious fur but have been reintroduced and are now recovering.

children.name: Pekania pennanti
children.description: A large, solitary mustelid of mature coniferous and mixed forests from the Sierra Nevada to Nova Scotia, notable as one of the very few predators capable of killing a porcupine. It was nearly extirpated by the fur trade and has been successfully reintroduced to many areas; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Ictonyx
children.description: The striped polecats of Africa — striking black-and-white patterned mustelids that, like skunks and zorillas, can eject a noxious musk from anal glands when threatened. The Striped Polecat is one of the most common and widespread small carnivores in Africa, familiar across both savanna and suburbia.

children.name: Ictonyx striatus
children.description: Africa's most widespread small carnivore, with bold black-and-white stripes running the length of the body and a dramatic warning display — it raises its tail and stands on its forelegs before spraying musk. It is bold, nocturnal, and omnivorous.

children.name: Ictonyx libycus
children.description: A smaller, paler striped polecat of the Saharan and Sahelian regions, with less contrasting stripes and a more desert-adapted lifestyle. It is poorly known compared to its widespread southern relative.

children.name: Poecilogale
children.description: A monotypic genus for the African Striped Weasel — a slender, black-and-white weasel of sub-Saharan grasslands that combines the body shape of a weasel with the defensive spray capability of a polecat. It is one of the smallest African carnivores and is remarkably powerful for its size, able to tackle prey much larger than itself.

children.name: Poecilogale albinucha
children.description: A beautiful, slender weasel with long white, black, and yellow stripes running along its back and a bushy white tail. It is a highly active hunter of small mammals and can climb and burrow effectively. It emits a pungent musk when threatened.

children.name: Lyncodon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Patagonian Weasel — a small, poorly known mustelid of the southern South American steppes that resembles a fusion of a weasel and a polecat. It was described by Charles Darwin during the Beagle voyage.

children.name: Lyncodon patagonicus
children.description: A small, secretive mustelid of the Patagonian steppes in Argentina and Chile, with a greyish body, dark brown legs, and a white stripe from the crown to the shoulders. It is thought to be a specialist rodent hunter but almost nothing is known of its behaviour or ecology.

children.name: Galictis
children.description: The grisons — two medium-sized mustelids of Central and South America with elongated bodies, short legs, and a distinctive grey 'saddle' pattern contrasting with black underparts. They are omnivorous and adaptable, inhabiting a wide range of forest and grassland habitats.

children.name: Galictis vittata
children.description: A medium-sized mustelid ranging from southern Mexico to Bolivia, with a grey back, black belly and legs, and a white stripe across the forehead and down the neck. It is omnivorous, feeding on small mammals, birds, fruits, and insects.

children.name: Galictis cuja
children.description: A smaller, more slender version of the Greater Grison, found from southern Peru to Patagonia. It is more carnivorous than its northern relative, with a diet dominated by small rodents and birds.

children.name: Eira
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Tayra — a large, long-tailed, tree-climbing mustelid of the Neotropics that resembles a giant marten. It is one of the most agile climbers of the family, capable of leaping between branches in the canopy with ease, and is equally at home on the ground.

children.name: Eira barbara
children.description: A large, dark mustelid of Neotropical forests from Mexico to Argentina, with a pale yellow to white patch on the throat and chest. It is diurnal, omnivorous, and highly intelligent — known to raid beehives and to imitate the calls of young agoutis to lure adults.

children.name: Melogale
children.description: Ferret-badgers — small, long-bodied mustelids of East and Southeast Asia with distinctive facial mask patterns and a strong musky odour. They are the most omnivorous of the mustelids, with a diet ranging from earthworms and insects to fruits and small vertebrates. Despite their name they are not true badgers but belong to a distinct subfamily of their own.

children.name: Melogale moschata
children.description: The most widespread ferret-badger, found across southern China, Taiwan, and northern Southeast Asia. It has a greyish-brown body, a white face with a black patch around each eye, and a prominent white stripe running from the crown to the rump.

children.name: Melogale personata
children.description: A ferret-badger of mainland Southeast Asia, distinguished from the Chinese Ferret-badger by a more extensive white facial stripe and darker body colour. It inhabits a variety of forest types up to moderate elevations.

children.name: Melogale orientalis
children.description: A ferret-badger endemic to Java and Bali in Indonesia, with a shorter tail and richer brown colour than its mainland relatives. It is common in rice fields and rural areas.

children.name: Melogale everetti
children.description: A poorly known ferret-badger endemic to the mountains of northern Borneo, with a distinctive dark face and reddish-brown body. It is the least known of all mustelids in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Melogale subaurantiaca
children.description: A ferret-badger endemic to Taiwan, distinguished by its rich golden-brown fur and smaller size. It is active at dusk and night, foraging in fields, forests, and along streams for earthworms and insects.
